data.headline: Why AI-Assisted Development — and Why Discipline Wins
data.subtext: The difference between a team that gets 2x and a team that ships slop is the operating discipline around the agent — not the model.

data.headline: [3 Days · 18 Hours]: Program Overview

rows.label: Audience
rows.content: LVT software developers — backend, frontend, full-stack and platform engineers, most new to AI-assisted dev tools. No prior agent experience assumed.

rows.label: Format
rows.content: 3 Days · 6 hrs/day · 18 Hrs · Fully Live · Hands-On Labs · Shared Sample Repo + Own Repos · Capstone

rows.label: Primary Tool
rows.content: Claude Code · Plan Mode · Subagents · Hooks · Skills / Plugins · MCP · OWASP Security Skills

rows.label: Outcome
rows.content: Developers who take a feature from spec to reviewed, tested and secure code using a repeatable Claude Code workflow they trust — and the patterns to scale it across the team.

rows.label: The 3-Day Arc
rows.content:
--Day 1 — Foundations & getting productive on a single repo
--Day 2 — Workflow discipline & the trust triad (anti-slop, adversarial review, secure-by-default)
--Day 3 — Scaling, automation & orchestration (skills, agent teams, hooks, advanced testing)

rows.label: Prerequisites
rows.content: Comfortable in a terminal and reading diffs · Git basics · Claude Code installed & authenticated · A non-production repo to work in · No prior AI-agent experience required

data.logos_note: Delivered by the world's Top AI practitioners from the global top AI companies and labs

data.headline: [3 Days, 18 Hours] — each day ends in a build, the programme ends in a capstone

data.headline: [Day 1]: Getting Productive on a Single Repo

rows.label: Objective
rows.content: Every developer can drive Claude Code through a real task in one repo with clean context and good prompting.

rows.label: Parts
rows.content:
--How coding agents actually work — prediction, context window, the agentic loop, when not to reach for them
--First contact with Claude Code — install, auth, the edit → run → review cycle, reading diffs
--Context management — what to put in, the cost of clutter, scoping & resetting
--Prompting for developers — specificity, success criteria, codifying worked examples to house style

rows.label: Tools
rows.content: Claude Code (CLI + IDE) · Shared sample repo · Context tooling · Diff review

rows.label: Hands-On
rows.content: Ship one small change end-to-end and open a PR · Rescue a task that fails with a bloated context · Rewrite three weak prompts and pin output to LVT house style.

rows.label: End of Day 1
rows.content:
--A merged (or PR-ready) end-to-end change on the sample repo
--A personal context-scoping checklist for their own repo
--Three rewritten prompts + a worked example codifying LVT style
--A working mental model of the agentic loop

data.headline: [Day 2]: Workflow Discipline & the Trust Triad
data.card_title: Day 2 — Spec to Secure, Reviewed Code

rows.label: Objective
rows.content: Take a feature from spec to reviewed, tested and secure code using a repeatable workflow developers trust.

rows.label: Parts
rows.content:
--Sizing, scoping & plan mode — match the approach to the task; correct the plan before code
--Spec-driven dev + CLAUDE.md — turn PRDs into executable specs; tune the repo's standing context
--Anti-slop & code review (1/3) — treat tidy first drafts as untrusted
--Adversarial multi-hat review (2/3) — security + design + black-box hats that debate
--Secure-by-default (3/3) — OWASP skills that catch issues as Claude writes
--Permissions — balance flow against safety

rows.label: The Trust Triad
rows.content: Anti-Slop Review · Adversarial Multi-Hat Review · Secure-by-Default

rows.label: Hands-On
rows.content: Drive a change through plan mode · Turn a PRD into a spec · Run a multi-hat review on a real diff · Install an OWASP skill that surfaces a planted vuln · Configure a permissions setup the team trusts.

rows.label: End of Day 2
rows.content:
--A PRD-to-spec-to-code workflow run in plan mode
--A tuned CLAUDE.md with before/after behaviour observed
--A multi-hat adversarial review with findings
--An OWASP secure-by-default setup that caught a vulnerability
--A written team workflow playbook

rows.label: Objective
rows.content: Understand the team-level tooling and orchestration features, and leave with the patterns LVT will standardise on. Exposure + reference — mostly demo-and-discuss.

rows.label: Blocks
rows.content:
--Reusable knowledge & extensions — writing skills, plugins, cross-tool sharing, multi-repo context
--Orchestration primitives — subagents · agent teams · dynamic workflows (who holds the plan?)
--Automation & guardrails — hooks (PreToolUse / PostToolUse) the whole team inherits
--Advanced testing now affordable — property-based, metamorphic, differential
--Working away from the keyboard — /loop & scheduled tasks, channels, remote control
--Long-term project memory + Capstone

rows.label: Tools
rows.content: Skills / Plugins · Subagents · Agent Teams · Dynamic Workflows · Hooks · MCP · Beads

rows.label: Capstone
rows.content: Small teams take a realistic feature from PRD → spec → plan → implementation → adversarial black-box + multi-hat review → human sign-off, wiring in at least one hook and one orchestration primitive.

rows.label: End of Day 3
rows.content:
--A working custom skill, shareable across Claude Code + Cursor
--Two committed hooks (auto-format + a dangerous-command guard)
--A clear map of subagents vs. agent teams vs. dynamic workflows for LVT
--A capstone feature taken to secure, reviewed, signed-off code

data.headline: The [Trust Triad]: how LVT developers ship AI-written code with confidence
data.card_1_title: Treat AI first drafts as untrusted
data.card_1_sub: They look tidy and idiomatic — which makes review harder, not easier
data.card_1_content:
--Anti-slop review — review for intent, not just style; never ship AI code with only AI-written tests
--Adversarial multi-hat review — a security hat, a design hat and a black-box hat that debate the tradeoffs
--Self-verifying loops — Claude runs builds, tests and lints to catch its own mistakes
data.card_2_sub: Catch issues while Claude writes — insecure code looks just as idiomatic
data.card_2_content:
--Secure-by-default OWASP skills applied as the code is written, with the 'why' explained
--Security review as a standard step — injection, IDOR, auth, crypto, plus OWASP for LLM / Agentic AI
--The marketplace is part of your attack surface — vet skills before installing
data.takeaway: The trust triad turns 'the AI wrote it' from a risk into a reviewed, secure, signed-off change.

data.headline: [What every developer builds] — concrete artifacts LVT can evidence for ROI
data.section_label: Produced live during the labs — recordable across the 3 days

cases.content:
End-to-End Feature | Shipped on a real repo via Claude Code
From edit → run → review to an opened pull request, with clean context and a house-style prompt.

Spec-to-Secure-Code Workflow | A trusted, repeatable pipeline
PRD → spec → plan-mode build → multi-hat review → OWASP secure-by-default → sign-off.

Team Guardrails | A tuned CLAUDE.md + committed hooks
Auto-format on edit and a PreToolUse guard the whole team inherits, plus a reusable skill.

Capstone Feature | PRD to signed-off code
Built by a small team with one hook and one orchestration primitive wired in — the proof of the workflow.

data.headline: How we deliver — and what we need from [LVT]
data.card_1_content:
--Every module ends in a lab on a shared sample repo plus participants' own repos
--Live pairing and code review — instructors review real diffs and prompts, not slides
--Customised to LVT's stack — languages, CI and security posture wired in at setup
--12–20 developers per cohort for effective live pairing
data.card_2_content:
--Developer laptops with Claude Code installed and authenticated
--A non-production (or sanitised) repository for hands-on work
--Tool versions and security skills confirmed before delivery
--Day-3 previews (agent teams, dynamic workflows, channels) are exposure + reference
data.takeaway: All tooling is customisable to align with LVT's existing infrastructure and security posture.
